a: Burhampoor

q: What did Passepartout buy at Burhampoor?
a: Indian slippers ornamented with false pearls

q: How many degrees west of Greenwich did Passepartout's watch show its London bias?
a: seventy-seven degrees westward

q: Why did Sir Francis insist Passepartout regulate his watch?
a: Going eastward (in the face of the sun), days are shorter by four minutes for each degree, so the watch had to be moved forward in each new meridian.

q: Where did the train stop because the railway wasn't finished?
a: the hamlet of Kholby

q: How many miles still separated Kholby from Allahabad?
a: fifty miles

q: What does Fogg say in response to the unfinished railway?
a: Not at all; but I knew that some obstacle or other would sooner or later arise on my route. Nothing, therefore, is lost. I have two days, which I have already gained, to sacrifice.

q: What conveyance does Passepartout find for the journey?
a: an elephant

q: What was the elephant's name?
a: Kiouni

q: Why was Kiouni only half-domesticated?
a: His owner had reared him for warlike purposes, irritating him and feeding him every three months on sugar and butter to impart a ferocity not in his nature.

q: What sums did Fogg offer to hire the elephant?
a: ten pounds an hour, then twenty, then forty — all refused

q: What price finally bought Kiouni outright?
a: two thousand pounds

q: Who guided the elephant?
a: a young Parsee with an intelligent face, an accomplished elephant driver

q: How were the riders distributed on the elephant?
a: Fogg and Sir Francis in howdahs on either flank, Passepartout astride the saddle-cloth between them, and the Parsee on the elephant's neck

q: At what time did Fogg's party set out from Kholby?
a: nine o'clock

q: What mountains separate the Khandeish from Bundelcund?
a: the Sutpour Mountains and the Vindhia Mountains

q: How many miles did the party gain by going through the forest?
a: twenty miles

q: What did Sir Francis exclaim about Kiouni after two hours?
a: Why, he's made of iron!

q: Passepartout's reply to Sir Francis about Kiouni?
a: Of forged iron

q: How did Passepartout treat Kiouni during the trek?
a: He gave him pieces of sugar from his pocket every so often, which Kiouni accepted without slackening his trot.

q: How far did the party travel the first day on the elephant?
a: nearly twenty-five miles

q: Where did the party halt on the first night?
a: in a ruined bungalow on the northern slope of the principal Vindhia chain

q: What deities or beasts disturbed the night at the bungalow?
a: occasional growls from panthers and chatterings of monkeys, but the more formidable beasts made no hostile demonstration

q: Whose procession was approaching through the forest at four o'clock?
a: a procession of Brahmins

q: Who is the goddess on the four-armed statue at the front of the procession?
a: the goddess Kali — goddess of love and death

q: What is suttee?
a: a human sacrifice — but a voluntary one — in which a widow is burned alive on her husband's funeral pyre

q: Who was the woman in the procession to be sacrificed?
a: the widow of an old rajah of Bundelcund

q: Was the sacrifice voluntary?
a: No — the guide insisted everyone in Bundelcund knew it was forced; she had been intoxicated with fumes of hemp and opium.

q: Where would the woman be taken to spend the night before the sacrifice?
a: the pagoda of Pillaji, two miles from the procession

q: What does Fogg propose to Sir Francis after the procession passes?
a: Suppose we save this woman.

q: How much time does Fogg say he has to spare for the rescue?
a: I have yet twelve hours to spare; I can devote them to that.

q: Sir Francis's reaction to Fogg's offer to save the widow?
a: Why, you are a man of heart!

q: Fogg's response to being called a man of heart?
a: Sometimes — when I have the time.

q: What was the name of the rescued princess?
a: Aouda

q: What is Aouda's family background?
a: She was a Parsee, the daughter of a wealthy Bombay merchant; she received a thoroughly English education and was a celebrated beauty of the Parsee race.

q: Why was Aouda being burned alive?
a: She was orphaned and married against her will to the old rajah of Bundelcund; on his death, the rajah's relatives — who had an interest in her death — devoted her to the suttee sacrifice.

q: Why did the guide agree to help save Aouda?
a: Officers, I am a Parsee, and this woman is a Parsee. Command me as you will.

q: What two means of getting at Aouda did the party consider?
a: entering through the doors while the Indians slept off their drunkenness, or making a hole through the temple walls

q: What tool did the rescuers have to break through the temple walls?
a: their pocket-knives

q: Why did the Parsee guide pick the rear walls?
a: no guards, and no windows or doors there; the brick-and-wood construction would yield to pocket-knives once the first brick was loosened

q: What ingenious trick did Passepartout pull off at the pagoda of Pillaji?
a: He slipped onto the pyre in the smoke, posed as the resuscitated rajah, lifted Aouda in his arms, and walked her out among the terror-stricken crowd.

q: What evidence revealed the trick had been discovered?
a: the cries and noise from the priests, and a ball that whizzed through Phileas Fogg's hat

q: Describe how Fogg rescues the Indian princess Aouda.
a: Fogg, Sir Francis Cromarty, and Passepartout intercept the funeral procession at Pillaji where Aouda is to be burned on her husband's pyre as a sati. Passepartout, having hidden among the corpses on the pyre, rises disguised as the dead rajah, terrifying the priests and bearers, allowing the group to escape with Aouda on the elephant Kiouni.

q: What did Fogg say to Passepartout about his pyre stunt?
a: Well done! — high commendation from Fogg

q: How does Passepartout joke about his role in the rescue?
a: For a few moments he, Passepartout, the ex-gymnast, ex-sergeant fireman, had been the spouse of a charming woman, a venerable, embalmed rajah.

q: What did Sir Francis say about Aouda's safety in India?
a: Should Aouda remain in India, she would inevitably fall again into the hands of her executioners — the fanatics were scattered throughout the country and would recover their victim at Madras, Bombay, or Calcutta.

q: What time did the party reach Allahabad station?
a: about ten o'clock

q: What does the name 'Allahabad' mean?
a: the City of God

q: At which sacred rivers' junction is Allahabad built?
a: the Ganges and the Jumna

q: How much did Passepartout pay for Aouda's wardrobe at Allahabad?
a: seventy-five pounds — a Scotch dress, a large mantle, and an otter-skin pelisse

q: Who was Ucaf Uddaul?
a: the poet-king who celebrated the charms of the queen of Ahmehnagara, whose verses are quoted to evoke Aouda's beauty

q: What does Verne emphasize about Aouda's manners?
a: She was a charming woman in all the European acceptation, spoke English with great purity, and had been transformed by her bringing up.

q: What did Fogg do with the elephant Kiouni?
a: He gave Kiouni to the Parsee guide as a reward for his devotion, on top of the agreed payment.

q: How did Kiouni react to Passepartout when given over?
a: The elephant grunted out his satisfaction, clasped Passepartout around the waist with his trunk, lifted him as high as his head, and replaced him gently on the ground.

q: How long is the train run from Allahabad to Benares?
a: eighty miles, accomplished in two hours

q: Where did Aouda hope to find her relative?
a: Hong Kong — a cousin named Jeejeeh

q: What was Fogg's offer when Aouda dreaded returning to India?
a: He offered to escort her to Hong Kong, where she could remain safely until the affair was hushed up — she eagerly and gratefully accepted.

q: What is Benares called by the Orientalists?
a: the Athens of India

q: What ancient city is Benares said to occupy the site of?
a: the ancient Casi

q: Whose troops was Sir Francis Cromarty rejoining at Benares?
a: his corps, encamped some miles northward of the city

q: Where is the principal opium market of India?
a: Patna

q: Which town is described as 'as English as Manchester or Birmingham'?
a: Monghir, with its iron foundries, edgetool factories, and high chimneys

q: Whose tomb stands on the left bank of the Ganges between Benares and Calcutta?
a: Lord Cornwallis

q: What time did the party reach Calcutta?
a: seven in the morning

q: When did the steamer for Hong Kong leave Calcutta?
a: noon

q: Was Fogg ahead of, behind, or on schedule at Calcutta?
a: Exactly on time — neither behind-hand nor ahead of his itinerary's October 25th.

q: Where did the gain of two days from London-to-Bombay get spent?
a: in the journey across India — particularly the unfinished railway and the elephant detour

q: Who arrests Fogg and Passepartout on arrival at Calcutta?
a: a policeman, who took them in a palkigahri to Judge Obadiah's court

q: What is a palkigahri?
a: a sort of four-wheeled carriage drawn by two horses

q: What two parts of Calcutta did the carriage pass through?
a: the 'black town' with narrow dirty streets, then the 'European town' with bright brick mansions and coconut palms

q: What is the name of the magistrate at Calcutta?
a: Judge Obadiah

q: Describe Judge Obadiah.
a: a fat, round man, who entered followed by his clerk and put on his wig hurriedly

q: What is the clerk's name in the Calcutta court?
a: Oysterpuff

q: What comic incident opens the trial?
a: Judge Obadiah grabbed the clerk's wig by mistake and exchanged it before pronouncing sentence.

q: Of what crime are Fogg and Passepartout accused at Calcutta?
a: having violated the sacred pagoda of Malabar Hill at Bombay on the 20th of October

q: Who arranged for the Bombay priests to bring suit against Passepartout in Calcutta?
a: Detective Fix, who had promised the priests a goodly sum in damages and sent them to Calcutta by the next train

q: What evidence did the clerk produce against Passepartout?
a: the desecrator's very shoes, which Passepartout had left behind at the pagoda

q: What sentence did Judge Obadiah hand down to Passepartout?
a: imprisonment for fifteen days and a fine of three hundred pounds

q: What sentence did Judge Obadiah hand down to Phileas Fogg?
a: a week's imprisonment and a fine of one hundred and fifty pounds

q: What did Fogg offer instead of accepting the sentence?
a: He offered bail.

q: How much was bail set at per prisoner?
a: one thousand pounds each

q: Total bail Fogg paid out at Calcutta?
a: two thousand pounds

q: Passepartout's wry remark about his shoes after the trial?
a: Ah, these are pretty dear shoes! More than a thousand pounds apiece; besides, they pinch my feet.

q: How much had Phileas Fogg already spent since leaving London by the time of the Calcutta bail?
a: more than five thousand pounds

q: On what items had Fogg's expenses accumulated up to Calcutta?
a: travelling expenses, bribes, the purchase of the elephant, bails, and fines

q: What was the name of the steamer that took Fogg from Calcutta to Hong Kong?
a: the Rangoon

q: How many hours ahead of departure did Fogg board the Rangoon?
a: an hour ahead — eleven o'clock was striking

q: What were the Rangoon's specifications?
a: Peninsular and Oriental Company screw steamer, iron, about 1,770 tons, 400 horsepower; fast but not as well fitted as the Mongolia

q: How long was the Calcutta–Hong Kong voyage?
a: about 3,500 miles, ten to twelve days

q: Who was Aouda's eminent Parsee relative who had been baronetted?
a: Sir Jametsee Jeejeebhoy

q: What was the name of Aouda's cousin in Hong Kong?
a: Jeejeeh

q: What word did Fogg use to assure Aouda everything would be arranged?
a: mathematically

q: What island did the Rangoon pass with Saddle Peak rising 2,400 feet?
a: the great Andaman, the principal of the islands in the Bay of Bengal

q: What does Verne explicitly deny about the Papuan inhabitants of the Andamans?
a: they are NOT cannibals, despite what has been asserted; they are merely in the lowest scale of humanity

q: What flora is described along the Andamans coast?
a: vast forests of palms, arecs, bamboo, teakwood, gigantic mimosa, and tree-like ferns

q: Where does the swallow's nest of Chinese tables come from?
a: the precious swallows of the Andaman coasts whose nests furnish a luxurious dish to the Celestial Empire

q: What straits gave access to the China seas?
a: the Straits of Malacca

q: Where was Fix planning to make the arrest if the warrant arrived?
a: Hong Kong — the last English ground; beyond it, China, Japan, and America offered an almost certain refuge

q: Why does Fix consider confiding in Passepartout?
a: He knew Passepartout was not Fogg's accomplice; if enlightened, the servant might become an ally and prevent Fogg's departure.

q: What new line of attack does Fix consider via Aouda?
a: He suspected a 'wicked elopement' and thought he could create such difficulties for Fogg at Hong Kong that Fogg could not escape by paying any amount.

q: What date is October 30th in the novel?
a: the day Fix decides to question Passepartout — the Rangoon was due at Singapore on the next day

q: Fix's cover for not being seen on the Rangoon since Calcutta?
a: He told Passepartout he'd been sea-sick — the Gulf of Bengal disagreed with him.

q: What does Fix offer Passepartout to maintain their cordiality?
a: a glass of gin

q: What conjecture does Passepartout settle on for Fix's identity?
a: that Fix is an agent of the Reform Club, sent to spy on Mr. Fogg and verify he really circumnavigates the globe

q: What date did the Rangoon enter the Strait of Malacca?
a: Wednesday, 30th October

q: When and where did the Rangoon weigh anchor for coal?
a: Singapore, the next day at four a.m.

q: How much time had Fogg gained by Singapore?
a: half a day on the prescribed time

q: What flora is conspicuous around Singapore?
a: palms with brilliant foliage, clove-trees, pepper plants, sago-bushes, large ferns, and nutmeg-trees

q: What animals did Aouda and Fogg observe in Singapore?
a: agile bands of monkeys in the trees, and tigers were not wanting in the jungles

q: What fruit did Passepartout buy several dozen of at Singapore?
a: mangoes — as large as good-sized apples, dark-brown outside and bright red within, with melting white pulp

q: Distance from Singapore to Hong Kong?
a: thirteen hundred miles

q: When did Fogg need to arrive at Hong Kong, and why?
a: by the 6th of November, to catch the steamer for Yokohama

q: What did Passepartout exclaim about the Rangoon's engine valves?
a: The valves are not sufficiently charged! We are not going. Oh, these English! If this was an American craft, we should blow up perhaps, but we should at all events go faster!

q: Why did Fix decide he must settle his approach with Passepartout?
a: He suspected Passepartout had guessed his real purpose, and could not allow Fogg to leave Hong Kong undetected if the warrant did not arrive.

q: What date did the squall hit the Rangoon?
a: the 3rd of November

q: What direction was the gale blowing from?
a: the north-west

q: By how many hours did the captain estimate the storm would delay the Rangoon?
a: twenty hours, perhaps more if the storm lasted

q: How did Fogg behave during the storm?
a: He gazed at the tempestuous sea with his habitual tranquillity — never changed countenance for an instant — as if the storm were part of his programme.

q: How did Fix respond to the storm?
a: It greatly pleased him — each delay filled him with hope that Fogg might be obliged to remain some days at Hong Kong; even sea-sickness did not dampen his exultation.

q: What did Passepartout do to vent his impatience during the storm?
a: He stayed on deck, lent a hand with the crew, pestered the captain and officers with questions, and shook the barometer in vain attempts to force it upwards.

q: When did the storm calm?
a: On the 4th the sea became more calm; the wind veered southward and was once more favourable.

q: By how many hours was Fogg behind on landing at Hong Kong?
a: twenty-four hours behind-hand

q: What ship was supposed to take Fogg from Hong Kong to Yokohama?
a: the Carnatic

q: Why was the Carnatic delayed at Hong Kong?
a: they had to repair one of her boilers, postponing departure

q: When was the Carnatic now scheduled to leave Hong Kong?
a: at high tide the next morning, originally five a.m.

q: Where did Aouda stay at Hong Kong while Fogg searched for Jeejeeh?
a: the Club Hotel

q: What did Fogg learn about Aouda's cousin Jeejeeh?
a: Jeejeeh had left China two years before, retired with an immense fortune, and taken up his residence in Europe — probably in Holland

q: What did Aouda decide to do upon learning Jeejeeh was in Holland?
a: She would go on to Europe with Fogg's party — Fogg insisted she did not embarrass his project.

q: How many cabins did Passepartout go to engage on the Carnatic?
a: three cabins — for Fogg, Aouda, and Passepartout

q: By what treaty did Hong Kong come into the possession of the English?
a: the Treaty of Nankin, after the war of 1842

q: How far is Hong Kong from the Portuguese town of Macao?
a: about sixty miles

q: What features make Hong Kong look like an English town?
a: docks, hospitals, wharves, a Gothic cathedral, a government house, and macadamised streets — an appearance like Kent or Surrey transferred to the antipodes

q: What colour are old Chinese men permitted to wear in Hong Kong, and from what age?
a: yellow — the Imperial colour, permitted from at least eighty years old

q: What change in the Carnatic's departure did Passepartout learn at the Hong Kong steamer office?
a: She would leave that very evening, not the next morning, because the boiler repairs had been completed.

q: What kind of place did Fix invite Passepartout into on the Hong Kong quay?
a: an opium-smoking tavern, where customers smoked long red clay pipes stuffed with opium balls mingled with essence of rose

q: How much opium do the English merchants sell to China each year, per the novel?
a: to the amount of one million four hundred thousand pounds

q: How long does an eight-pipe-a-day opium smoker last?
a: he dies in five years

q: Whom did Fix reveal himself to be at the opium tavern?
a: a London police detective on Mr. Fogg's track for the Bank of England robbery

q: How much was the Bank of England reward Fix offered to share with Passepartout?
a: two thousand pounds — Fix offered Passepartout five hundred

q: Did Passepartout agree to help Fix?
a: No — he refused outright. 'I have seen his generosity and goodness; and I will never betray him — not for all the gold in the world. I come from a village where they don't eat that kind of bread!'

q: What did Fix do to Passepartout after the refusal?
a: He slipped an opium pipe into Passepartout's hand; Passepartout drew puffs and slumped unconscious on the table.

q: What was Fix's plan after Passepartout passed out?
a: Fogg would not be informed of the Carnatic's earlier departure, and would have to leave without the cursed Frenchman.

q: What was the date of the Bank of England robbery, per Fix's revelation?
a: the 28th of last September

q: Describe Fogg's manner with Aouda on the Rangoon.
a: He visited her regularly each day at certain hours, not so much to talk himself as to sit and hear her talk — strict politeness, with the precision of an automaton — neither voice nor manner betrayed the slightest emotion.

q: What does the narrator imply Fogg would calculate while looking at Sir Francis?
a: the number of hours spent since his departure from London — and, had it been in his nature, would have rubbed his hands for satisfaction

q: What gave the Bundelcund region its lawless character?
a: Inaccessible mountain rajahs and a fanatical population hardened in the most horrible practices of the Hindu faith — the English had been unable to secure complete dominion.

q: Where on the pyre was Aouda placed during the rescue?
a: stretched out beside her husband's body, fifty paces from the rescuers, with the wood heavily soaked with oil

q: How did Aouda first learn of her rescue?
a: Sir Francis narrated to her what had passed during the train run, dwelling upon Fogg's courage; Mr. Fogg said nothing, while Passepartout, abashed, kept repeating that 'it wasn't worth telling.'

q: What was the Rangoon's location when Fogg boarded?
a: moored half a mile off in the Calcutta harbour, signal of departure already hoisted at the mast-head

q: How does Fogg compare to a 'disturbing star' in the narrator's metaphor?
a: He passed methodically in his orbit around the world, regardless of the lesser stars gravitating around him; Aouda's charms — what astronomers would call a disturbing star — failed to act on him.

q: Why was Mr. Fogg so calm during the storm?
a: It was not because he had foreseen this particular storm but because nothing — earth, sea, sky — could be allowed to disturb the man whose programme included 'all included' for unforeseens.

q: What was Passepartout's reaction when Fix told him Mr. Fogg had stolen 55,000 pounds?
a: He struck the table with his fist and cried 'What nonsense! My master is the most honourable of men!'

q: What did Sir Francis say at parting from Fogg in Benares?
a: He bade adieu to Phileas Fogg, wishing him all success and expressing the hope that he would come that way again in a less original but more profitable fashion.

q: What habit kept Fogg in his cabin most of the Rangoon voyage?
a: He kept Aouda company, or, according to his inveterate habit, took a hand at whist.

q: What was the lighting condition at the rescue moment?
a: The night was dark; the moon, on the wane, scarcely left the horizon and was covered with heavy clouds; the height of the trees deepened the darkness.

q: Why couldn't Passepartout bribe the engineer to go faster?
a: While bribery could hasten a steamer, it could not be done on the railway.

q: What were the fakirs doing around the Kali statue in the procession?
a: capering and making a wild ado round the statue, striped with ochre and covered with cuts whence their blood issued drop by drop

q: What did Aouda offer to do upon being arrested with Fogg?
a: She told Fogg, 'Sir, you must leave me to my fate! It is on my account that you receive this treatment, it is for having saved me!' — Fogg refused outright.

q: What is the date that the priests at Pillaji had set for the sacrifice?
a: the dawn following the procession's arrival at the pagoda

q: What instrument did Judge Obadiah pull down from a nail?
a: a wig — but he initially grabbed his clerk's wig by mistake

q: What feature spared Fogg from a longer Hong Kong delay?
a: Chance — had not the Carnatic been forced to lie over for boiler repairs, she would have left on the 6th, leaving Fogg to wait a week for the next steamer.

q: How does Hong Kong's appearance reflect English influence?
a: Streets of macadam, a Gothic cathedral, government house, hospitals, wharves, ships of all nations and Chinese junks — like a town in Kent or Surrey transferred by some strange magic to the antipodes.

q: What words did Mr. Fogg use to give Kiouni to the guide?
a: Parsee, you have been serviceable and devoted. I have paid for your service, but not for your devotion. Would you like to have this elephant? He is yours.

q: Was the time Fogg arrived in Calcutta consistent with his itinerary?
a: Exactly — neither behind nor ahead. The two days gained between London and Bombay had been lost in the journey across India, but he was on schedule.

q: What is the size and population context for Hong Kong as described by the narrator?
a: Wholly an English city, on an island near the Chinese coast — Aouda's relative was a rich merchant there

q: How did the captain make use of favourable wind?
a: He put up his sails, and under the double action of steam and sail the vessel made rapid progress along the coasts of Anam and Cochin China.

q: What document did Fix display to convince Passepartout he was a detective?
a: his commission from the London office — Passepartout was speechless with astonishment, the genuineness of which could not be doubted

q: What word did Fogg use to express the foreseen risk before the rescue?
a: That is foreseen — replied Mr. Fogg about the risk of horrible tortures if caught

q: What did Fogg propose, after Sir Francis warned him about Passepartout's pagoda offence?
a: He calmly observed that even if his servant had been caught and condemned, the master would not have been delayed; Passepartout would have quietly returned to Europe.

q: Did Fix witness the Calcutta court proceedings?
a: Yes — he was ensconced in a corner of the court-room watching, pleased that Fogg might be detained, but disappointed when Fogg paid the bail.

q: Who was the famous Parsee baronet referenced in the novel?
a: Sir Jametsee Jeejeebhoy — made baronet by the English government for his Bombay cotton fortune

q: What does the narrator say about Treaty of Nankin and the Hong Kong harbour?
a: Hong Kong's port came into English possession by the Treaty of Nankin (1842); the colonising genius of the English created upon it an important city and excellent port — Victoria port — full of English, French, American, Dutch ships, junks, sempas, tankas, and flower-boats.

q: What was the consequence for Fogg if the storm delay caused him to miss the Yokohama boat?
a: It would almost inevitably cause the loss of the wager — but he manifested neither impatience nor annoyance.

q: Why did the goddess Kali statue appear ugly to Passepartout?
a: He could accept her as goddess of death but not of love — 'that ugly old hag? Never!'

q: What detail did Verne add about the goddess Kali sectaries that crossed the Khandeish?
a: These sectaries strangled victims of every age in honour of the goddess Death without ever shedding blood — there was a period when the country could scarcely be travelled without corpses being found in every direction.

q: What does Passepartout call Fix in the bantering tone?
a: a sly dog

q: How does Passepartout learn about Fix's continued presence on the Rangoon?
a: Fix emerges from his cabin and pretends extreme surprise at finding Passepartout aboard, claiming sea-sickness as the cover for not having been seen since Calcutta.

q: What did Mr. Fogg do all evening before his expected steamer departure at Hong Kong?
a: He absorbed himself in the perusal of the Times and Illustrated London News.

q: Why did the Carnatic leave Hong Kong twelve hours early?
a: the repairs on her boilers had been completed sooner than expected

q: What was Fogg's reaction on learning the Carnatic had sailed without him?
a: It is an accident, madam; nothing more.

q: Who was the master of the Tankadere?
a: John Bunsby

q: What number was the pilot-boat that took Fogg from Hong Kong toward Yokohama?
a: No. 43

q: What was the Tankadere's tonnage?
a: twenty tons

q: What speed was the Tankadere capable of?
a: between eight and nine knots an hour

q: Describe the Tankadere's appearance.
a: a neat little craft of twenty tons, gracefully built like a racing yacht, with shining copper sheathing, galvanised iron-work, deck white as ivory, and two masts leaning a trifle backward

q: What sails did the Tankadere carry?
a: brigantine, foresail, storm-jib, and standing-jib

q: How many crew members did John Bunsby have?
a: four hardy mariners familiar with the Chinese seas

q: What price did Fogg offer for the trip to Yokohama?
a: one hundred pounds per day plus an additional reward of two hundred pounds if he reached Yokohama in time

q: Why couldn't the Tankadere reach Yokohama?
a: It was 1,660 miles from Hong Kong, too far for so small a boat at that time of year.

q: What route did Bunsby propose instead?
a: going to Shanghai — only 800 miles from Hong Kong, kept close to the Chinese coast where currents run northward and aid the boat

q: Why did Fogg need the steamer at Shanghai, not Yokohama?
a: The San Francisco steamer started from Shanghai and only stopped at Yokohama and Nagasaki along the way.

q: When did the San Francisco steamer leave Shanghai?
a: on the 11th, at seven in the evening

q: How many hours did Fogg have to reach Shanghai?
a: ninety-six hours — four days

q: How much earnest money did Fogg pay Bunsby up front?
a: two hundred pounds on account

q: What did Fogg do for Passepartout while at Hong Kong?
a: He left a description of the missing servant at the Hong Kong police station and the French consulate, with a sum of money to be spent in the search.

q: What flag was hoisted on the Tankadere when she sailed?
a: the English flag

q: When did the Tankadere depart Hong Kong?
a: ten minutes past three

q: What time of year were the Chinese seas crossing happening?
a: early November — equinox season, terrible gales of wind

q: What straits did the Tankadere enter past the Chinese coast?
a: the Straits of Fo-Kien, separating Formosa from the Chinese coast

q: What latitude line did the Tankadere cross in the small hours?
a: the Tropic of Cancer

q: What direction was the typhoon coming from?
a: south

q: Fogg's reaction to the typhoon coming from the south?
a: Glad it's a typhoon from the south, for it will carry us forward.

q: What sail configuration did Bunsby adopt for the typhoon?
a: all sails reefed, pole-masts dispensed with; a single triangular storm-jib of strong canvas to hold wind from behind

q: What was the comparison given for the Tankadere's typhoon-driven speed?
a: more than four times that of a locomotive going on full steam — and even that was below the truth

q: When the wind veered NW, what did Bunsby propose?
a: to make for one of the ports on the coast — Shanghai, since it was the only port Fogg knew of

q: How did the Tankadere signal the American steamer at Shanghai?
a: by hoisting the flag at half-mast (signal of distress) and firing a small brass cannon loaded to the muzzle

q: How far from the mouth of Shanghai River was the Tankadere at six o'clock?
a: ten miles — and Shanghai itself is twelve miles up the stream

q: How offset was Fogg's wager when the American steamer first appeared?
a: Three miles from Shanghai, with the steamer leaving — the £200 reward was on the point of escaping; yet Fogg was perfectly tranquil, his whole fortune at stake.

q: What did Bunsby cry when he saw the American steamer leaving?
a: Confound her!

q: When did the Carnatic actually depart Hong Kong?
a: half-past six on the 7th of November

q: How did Passepartout end up on the Carnatic?
a: He awoke from his opium stupor three hours after Fix left, was driven by the fixed idea of the Carnatic, staggered along walls, fell unconscious onto the deck just as the steamer moved off, and was carried to a second-class cabin by sailors.

q: How far had the Carnatic gone before Passepartout woke?
a: one hundred and fifty miles from China

q: When did the Carnatic reach Yokohama?
a: at dawn on the 13th of November

q: In what bay is Yokohama situated?
a: the bay of Yeddo

q: Who was the civil Emperor of Japan referenced in the novel?
a: the Tycoon — until the Mikado, the spiritual Emperor, absorbed his office

q: What is the Japanese quarter of Yokohama called?
a: Benten — named after the goddess of the sea

q: Which sects sheltered in Benten's holy retreats?
a: Buddhist priests and sectaries of Confucius

q: What is saki?
a: a liquor concocted from the fermentation of rice, served in tea-houses with tea

q: What is the Japanese view of opium, per Verne?
a: almost unknown in Japan — the smoking-houses use a very fine, stringy tobacco instead

q: Why did Passepartout find no meat at Yokohama butchers?
a: It is a sacrilege to kill cattle, which are preserved solely for farming.

q: What types of trees do the Japanese cultivate for blossoms rather than fruit?
a: cherry, plum, and apple

q: Which bird is sacred in Japan, symbolising long life and prosperity?
a: the crane

q: What did Passepartout try to eat when he saw violets among the shrubs?
a: He hoped to make supper of them, but they were odourless.

q: How did Passepartout describe the police and custom-house officers' attire?
a: pointed hats encrusted with lac, two sabres at their waists

q: How are Japanese soldiers dressed compared with Mikado's guards?
a: Japanese soldiers wear blue cotton with white stripes; the Mikado's guards wear silken doubles, hauberks, and coats of mail.

q: How does Verne contrast the standing of military profession in Japan vs. China?
a: as much respected in Japan as it is despised in China

q: What did Passepartout chuckle at when watching patrols pass at night in Yokohama?
a: He thought the patrol officers in their splendid costumes looked like ambassadors, and joked 'another Japanese embassy departing for Europe!'

q: How did Passepartout get a meal in Yokohama?
a: He exchanged his European costume with a native old-clothes dealer for an old Japanese coat and a one-sided turban, plus a few silver pieces; he then breakfasted at a tea-house on half a bird and a little rice.

q: What was Passepartout's plan to cross the Pacific to America?
a: He intended to offer himself as a cook or servant on a steamer in payment of his passage and meals.

q: How many miles is the Pacific between Japan and America, per the novel?
a: four thousand seven hundred miles

q: Whose acrobatic troupe gave Passepartout employment?
a: the Honourable William Batulcar's troupe

q: How was Batulcar described?
a: a sort of Barnum, the director of a troupe of mountebanks, jugglers, clowns, acrobats, equilibrists, and gymnasts

q: Under whose patronage were the Long Noses?
a: the god Tingou

q: How long were the Long Noses' bamboo noses?
a: five, six, and even ten feet long, some straight, others curved, ribboned, with imitation warts

q: What was the climactic stunt of the show?
a: a 'human pyramid' of fifty Long Noses representing the Car of Juggernaut, with artists grouped on top of the noses themselves rather than each other's shoulders

q: Why was Passepartout chosen to be in the troupe?
a: the previous performer who formed the base of the Car had quit; only strength and adroitness were necessary, plus he could sing and was strong

q: What length nose did Passepartout fasten on?
a: six feet long

q: What collapsed Passepartout's pyramid stunt?
a: He saw Mr. Fogg in the gallery, abandoned his position, cleared the footlights without his wings, and fell at the feet of his master.

q: How did Fogg appease Mr. Batulcar after the pyramid 'breakage'?
a: by giving him a handful of banknotes for damages

q: At what time did Fogg's party board the American steamer at Yokohama?
a: half-past six — the very hour of departure, with Passepartout still wearing his wings and six-foot nose

q: What additional sum did Fogg pay John Bunsby on top of the agreed price?
a: five hundred and fifty pounds

q: Where did Bunsby's signal-receivers take Fogg's party first?
a: to Nagasaki and then Yokohama

q: When did Fogg's party reach Yokohama?
a: on the morning of the 14th of November

q: What was the name of the Pacific Mail Steamship Company vessel?
a: the General Grant

q: What were the General Grant's specifications?
a: a large paddle-wheel steamer of two thousand five hundred tons, well equipped and very fast, three-masted

q: How fast did the General Grant cross the Pacific?
a: twelve miles an hour, crossing the ocean in twenty-one days

q: When did Fogg expect to reach San Francisco?
a: by the 2nd of December

q: When was Fogg expected at New York?
a: the 11th of December

q: When was Fogg expected back at London?
a: the 20th of December — a few hours before the deadline of the 21st

q: What date did the General Grant cross the 180th meridian?
a: the 23rd of November

q: How many of the 80 days had Fogg used by November 23?
a: fifty-two of the eighty — twenty-eight remaining

q: What is the actual distance Fogg would travel vs. straight-line distance?
a: twenty-six thousand miles actual vs. about twelve thousand miles along the fiftieth parallel

q: How many miles had Fogg traveled by November 23rd?
a: seventeen thousand five hundred

q: Why did Passepartout's London-time watch suddenly agree with the ship's chronometers?
a: He had crossed half the globe — a 12-hour offset that, on his 12-hour-face watch, looked like correct time, though on a 24-hour clock it would still be off.

q: What did Fix discover at Yokohama?
a: the warrant of arrest had finally arrived via the Carnatic, but it was now useless — Fogg was off English soil, and extradition was needed

q: How did Passepartout react to seeing Fix on the General Grant?
a: He grasped Fix by the throat and administered a perfect volley of blows — to the great amusement of a group of Americans, who began to bet on him.

q: After the fight, how did Fix propose to Passepartout?
a: He proposed switching sides — he would now help Fogg complete his journey to England, where the warrant would be valid and Fogg could be arrested on home soil.

q: What did Passepartout reply when Fix asked if they were friends?
a: Friends? — no — but allies, perhaps. At the least sign of treason, however, I'll twist your neck for you.

q: When did the General Grant reach San Francisco?
a: the 3rd of December — Mr. Fogg had neither gained nor lost a single day

q: What time did Fogg's party land at San Francisco?
a: seven in the morning

q: What time did the first New York train depart San Francisco?
a: six o'clock p.m.

q: What did the carriage cost from the wharf?
a: three dollars

q: Where did Fogg's party stay in San Francisco?
a: the International Hotel

q: What is Montgomery Street to San Francisco?
a: what Regent Street is to London, the Boulevard des Italiens to Paris, and Broadway to New York

q: What political meeting was happening on Montgomery Street?
a: an election — actually for a justice of the peace, between Camerfield and Mandiboy

q: Who was the bully who threatened Fogg at the rally?
a: Colonel Stamp Proctor — a big, brawny fellow with a red beard, flushed face, and broad shoulders

q: Who saved Fogg from Colonel Proctor's punch?
a: Fix — he rushed in and received the blow in Fogg's stead, getting an enormous bruise under his silk hat

q: How did Fogg respond to Proctor's threat?
a: Yankee! — and Fogg vowed to come back to America to call him to account

q: What was the actual purpose of the political rally?
a: an election — but not for governor or member of Congress: for a justice of the peace

q: What did Passepartout buy in San Francisco?
a: half a dozen six-barrelled revolvers — Enfield rifles and Colt's revolvers — concerned about Sioux and Pawnee attacks on trains

q: Did Mr. Fogg approve Passepartout's gun purchase?
a: He thought it a useless precaution but told him to do as he thought best.

q: What is the Pacific Railroad's general designation per the Americans?
a: From ocean to ocean — the great trunk line crossing the entire United States

q: What two railroad companies make up the Pacific Railroad?
a: the Central Pacific (San Francisco-Ogden) and the Union Pacific (Ogden-Omaha)

q: What is the total mileage of the Pacific Railroad to New York?
a: three thousand seven hundred and eighty-six miles

q: How many days did the New York-San Francisco journey take post-railroad?
a: seven days — formerly took six months at minimum

q: Between which parallels was the Pacific Railroad laid?
a: the forty-first and forty-second parallels

q: Who fixed the line's eastern end at Omaha, Nebraska?
a: President Lincoln himself, in 1862

q: How fast did the railroad grow on the prairies?
a: a mile and a half a day

q: What was the maximum grade of the railroad on the Rocky Mountains?
a: 112 feet to the mile

q: Describe the railway car interior.
a: a long omnibus on eight wheels with no compartments, two rows of seats perpendicular to the train direction, an aisle to platforms; saloon, balcony, restaurant, and smoking-cars throughout the train

q: When did the first train depart Oakland for Omaha?
a: six o'clock p.m.

q: How fast did the train go counting stoppages?
a: no more than twenty miles an hour

q: How was the car converted into a dormitory at night?
a: The seat backs were thrown back, bedsteads carefully packed were rolled out, and berths with thick curtains, clean sheets, and soft pillows were improvised.

q: What time did the train reach Sacramento?
a: around midnight

q: How many miles between San Francisco and Sacramento?
a: one hundred and twenty miles, accomplished in six hours

q: Where was 'Cisco reached, and at what time?
a: at seven in the morning, in the Sierra Nevada range

q: Where did the train stop for breakfast at midday?
a: Reno — twenty minutes for breakfast

q: What size buffalo herd halted the train, and for how long?
a: ten or twelve thousand head, for three full hours

q: Why didn't the engineer plough through the buffalo?
a: He would crush the first ones, but the locomotive — however powerful — would soon be checked, the train thrown off the track, and become helpless.

q: How did Fogg react to the buffalo delay?
a: He waited philosophically until it should please the buffaloes to get out of the way.

q: What time did the train enter Utah?
a: half-past nine in the evening

q: Who was the Mormon missionary aboard the train?
a: Elder William Hitch

q: Where did Elder Hitch deliver his Mormon lecture?
a: car No. 117, on train No. 48, from eleven to twelve o'clock

q: How many people initially attended Elder Hitch's lecture?
a: about thirty out of one hundred passengers; the count steadily diminished to ten, and finally to one (Passepartout)

q: Whom did Elder Hitch declare a martyr at the start?
a: Joe Smith and his brother Hiram, with the persecutions against the prophets making a martyr of Brigham Young

q: From where did Joseph Smith Jr. claim the annals of the Mormon religion?
a: He was a Vermont farmer who, in 1825, revealed himself as a mystical prophet, when a celestial messenger appeared to him in an illuminated forest and gave him the annals of the Lord — a translation of an ancient Egyptian book.

q: What town did the Mormons build in Ohio with their temple?
a: Kirkland

q: Cost of the Kirkland Mormon temple?
a: two hundred thousand dollars

q: What did Smith's ruined creditors do to him in 1837?
a: they gave him a coat of tar and feathers

q: Where did Smith reappear after his bankruptcy?
a: Independence, Missouri — chief of a flourishing colony of three thousand disciples

q: Where did Smith found a community in 1839?
a: Nauvoo, on the Mississippi, in Illinois — twenty-five thousand souls

q: What political ambition did Smith announce in 1843?
a: He announced himself as a candidate for the Presidency of the United States.

q: How was Joseph Smith killed?
a: drawn into ambuscade at Carthage, thrown into prison, and assassinated by a band of men disguised in masks

q: Who was Joseph Smith's successor?
a: Brigham Young, who two years later led the Mormons from Nauvoo to the banks of the Great Salt Lake

q: What is the Great Salt Lake's size?
a: seventy miles long and thirty-five wide, three miles eight hundred feet above the sea

q: What proportion of the Great Salt Lake water is solid matter?
a: one quarter of the weight

q: Why can't fish live in the Great Salt Lake?
a: Its salinity is too high; specific weight 1,170 — fish that descend through the Jordan, Weber, and other streams soon perish.

q: How long did the train rest at Ogden?
a: six hours

q: What did Fogg's party do at Ogden?
a: They paid a visit to Salt Lake City via a branch road, spending two hours there.

q: What surrounded Salt Lake City?
a: a clay and pebble wall built in 1853

q: What characterizes Salt Lake City's layout?
a: the Anglo-Saxon taste for symmetry — built like a checker-board, 'with the sombre sadness of right-angles' (Victor Hugo)

q: What buildings in Salt Lake City did Verne describe?
a: the prophet's mansion, the court-house, the arsenal, blue-brick houses with verandas and porches, surrounded by gardens with acacias, palms, and locusts

q: Are all Mormons polygamists, per Verne?
a: No — Mormons are free to marry or not. But mainly female citizens of Utah want to marry, since maiden ladies are not admitted to the highest joys of the Mormon religion.

q: What was the belated Mormon's answer to Passepartout's question of how many wives he had?
a: One, sir — one, and that was enough!

q: How far was the train from San Francisco at Weber River?
a: nearly nine hundred miles

q: What was the government subsidy for laying track in the Wahsatch-Rockies region?
a: forty-eight thousand dollars per mile, instead of sixteen thousand allowed for plain work

q: How long was the only tunnel pierced in the Wahsatch-Rockies section?
a: fourteen thousand feet

q: When did the train stop at Fort Bridger?
a: ten o'clock at night

q: When did the train enter Wyoming Territory?
a: twenty minutes after Fort Bridger

q: What was the date of the Green River station stop?
a: the 7th of December

q: Whom did Aouda recognize at Green River station?
a: Colonel Stamp Proctor, the same who had insulted Fogg at the San Francisco meeting

q: What was Aouda's worry about the Proctor encounter?
a: Mr. Fogg would call Proctor to account; a duel would delay or kill him.

q: How did Fix and Passepartout plan to keep Fogg from seeing Proctor?
a: by getting him absorbed in a four-handed whist game with Aouda, Fix, and a dummy — Fogg was naturally sedentary, so this would work

q: What was Bridger Pass's elevation?
a: seven thousand five hundred and twenty-four feet above sea level — one of the highest points the track reached

q: What named the engineer who proposed the bridge stunt?
a: Forster — a true Yankee

q: What was wrong with the Medicine Bow bridge?
a: It was a suspension-bridge over rapids about a mile away, in a ruinous condition with several iron wires broken — the signalman said it would not bear the weight of the train.

q: What was the engineer Forster's proposal?
a: Put on the very highest speed and have a chance of getting over — about fifty out of a hundred (one passenger boasted eighty or ninety).

q: What was Passepartout's prudent counter-proposal?
a: More natural for us to cross the bridge on foot, and let the train come after.

q: How fast did the train go to clear the Medicine Bow bridge?
a: a hundred miles an hour — they perceived the train hardly bore upon the rails at all

q: What happened to the bridge after the train passed?
a: It collapsed completely with a crash into the rapids of Medicine Bow.

q: What weather threatened the train route after Green River?
a: Snow had fallen abundantly during the night but, mixed with rain, it had half melted and did not interrupt progress.

q: Where did Fix hide on the General Grant before being recognized?
a: in his cabin, hoping to remain unperceived by Fogg's servant

q: What did Fix and Fogg do after the political brawl on Montgomery Street?
a: They went to a tailor's — both their clothing was in rags — and an hour later returned to the International Hotel suitably attired.

q: In what year was the Pacific Railroad work commenced?
What state had the Mormons fled from in 1845?
a: Illinois

q: What did Elder Hitch say about the lands the Mormons had been driven from?
a: Driven from Vermont, driven from Illinois, driven from Ohio, driven from Missouri, driven from Utah, we shall yet find some independent territory on which to plant our tents.

q: How did Passepartout impress Mr. Batulcar enough to join the troupe?
a: He was a Frenchman known for grimaces, was strong, could sing, and recalled the gymnastic exercises of his younger days — including singing standing on his head with a spinning top.

q: Why did Passepartout react with thunderstruck despair on the Carnatic?
a: He realized he had failed to inform Fogg of the steamer's earlier departure, and he and Aouda had missed their boat — Fogg's wager was probably ruined.

q: What had Fix done previously to detain Fogg, per his confession to Passepartout?
a: He sent the Bombay priests after Fogg, got Passepartout intoxicated at Hong Kong, and made him miss the Yokohama steamer.

q: What two-day grace period did Passepartout have on the Carnatic?
a: His passage had fortunately been paid in advance; he had five or six days to figure out his next steps in Japan.

q: How long did the typhoon last on the Tankadere?
a: from about eight in the evening through the next day — brief but terrific; some signs of calm at noon the next day

q: What scarecrows did Passepartout see in the Yokohama countryside?
a: queerly-fashioned, grinning scarecrows protecting cherry, plum, and apple trees from sparrows, pigeons, ravens, and other voracious birds

q: What is the elevation peak of the Pacific Railroad's grade in the Rockies?
a: 112 feet to the mile maximum grade — even on the Rockies

q: Why did Fix want Mr. Fogg back living in Europe?
a: Fix had switched sides — he would do anything to get Fogg back to England where the warrant was valid; only there could he arrest him.

q: What was the dummy in Fogg's whist game?
a: A standard whist convention — a phantom fourth player; Fogg had three real partners (Aouda, Fix, and presumably himself).

q: Who started the bridge-jumping argument with Passepartout's caution?
a: Colonel Stamp Proctor — the word 'prudent' seemed to excite him prodigiously: 'At full speed, don't you see, at full speed!'

q: What did Passepartout's challenge to Proctor end with?
a: Are you afraid? — to which Passepartout replied 'I afraid? Very well; I will show these people that a Frenchman can be as American as they!'

q: Where did Fogg's party have their first meal in San Francisco?
a: the International Hotel — dried beef, oyster soup, biscuits, and cheese; payment only for ale, porter, or sherry

q: What did Passepartout say when Elder Hitch invited him to plant his tent under the Mormon flag?
a: No! — and he retired from the car, leaving the Elder to preach to vacancy.

q: What route across America did Passepartout's plan rely on?
a: Once at San Francisco he would find some means of going on — the difficulty being how to traverse the four thousand seven hundred miles of Pacific between Japan and the New World.

q: What pattern did Bunsby see on the horizon predicting the typhoon?
a: the sun had set the evening before in a red mist amid phosphorescent scintillations; the barometer rose and fell capriciously; the south-east raised long surges

q: Who said 'Have you done?' after the Passepartout-Fix fight?
a: Fix — getting up in a somewhat rumpled condition

q: How does Verne describe Aouda's growing feelings on the Pacific?
a: She felt herself more and more attached to him by other ties than gratitude — his silent but generous nature impressed her more than she thought, and she yielded almost unconsciously to emotions which did not seem to have the least effect upon her protector.

q: How was the Pacific Railroad usually described by Americans?
a: From ocean to ocean — these four words compose the general designation

q: What is the depth of Lake Asphaltite for comparison?
a: twelve hundred feet below the sea — the Great Salt Lake by contrast is three miles eight hundred feet ABOVE the sea

q: What did Aouda fear about the Mr. Fogg-Proctor encounter?
a: Mr. Fogg would allow no one to avenge him; if he saw Proctor, a duel could ruin everything — whether victorious or beaten, he would be delayed.

q: What happened to the Tankadere when the wind veered?
a: She lay in the trough of the waves and shook and rolled terribly; the sea struck her with fearful violence.

q: Why was Passepartout 'put out of countenance' on landing in San Francisco?
a: He attempted a perilous vault on the floating quay, fell through some worm-eaten planks, and let out a loud cry that frightened cormorants and pelicans on the movable quays.

q: What's the difference between Passepartout's perception of San Francisco and the legendary 1849 city?
a: It was no longer a paradise of outlaws gambling with gold-dust, with revolver and bowie-knife — it was now a great commercial emporium with City Hall tower, broad avenues, and Anglo-Saxon Gothic churches.

q: What did the engineer plan to ensure the bridge would hold long enough?
a: He reversed steam, backed the train nearly a mile to take a longer leap, then drove forward at full speed — the rapidity becoming frightful, twenty piston strokes a second.

q: What does Verne say about the level of Mormons' institutions vs. their people?
a: the people are certainly not up to the level of their institutions — everything is done 'squarely' (cities, houses, follies)

q: What did Passepartout buy in San Francisco for the train journey?
a: half a dozen six-barrelled revolvers, in case of attacks by the Sioux and Pawnees

q: Why did Passepartout hide his Hong Kong story from Fogg upon reunion?
a: He simply excused himself for being overtaken by drunkenness from smoking opium at a Hong Kong tavern, and decided the time had not yet come to divulge what had passed between him and the detective.

q: What reflections did Fix have on the Tankadere about Fogg's strategy?
a: Instead of sailing directly from England to the United States like a common villain, Fogg had traversed three quarters of the globe to gain the American continent more surely — and there could quietly enjoy himself with the bank fortune.

q: What did Mr. Fogg say to John Bunsby once the boat got into open sea?
a: I do not need, pilot, to advise you to use all possible speed.

q: What sort of beasts and people inhabit the Omaha-Pacific stretch?
a: still infested by Indians and wild beasts, plus a large tract that the Mormons (driven from Illinois in 1845) began to colonise

q: What annual cost did the U.S. government pay to subdue the Mormons?
a: Verne does not give a dollar figure; he says the government had just succeeded with some difficulty in reducing these independent fanatics to its rule, after imprisoning Brigham Young.

q: What metaphor does Verne use for Fogg in his orbit?
a: He passed methodically in his orbit around the world, regardless of the lesser stars which gravitated around him; Aouda was the disturbing star whose charms failed to act.

q: What was the size of Colonel Proctor's bruise to Fix?
a: an enormous bruise immediately appeared under the detective's silk hat, which was completely smashed in

q: What kind of architecture, dancers, and flora did Yokohama's Benten quarter contain?
a: fir and cedar groves, sacred gates of singular architecture, bridges hidden in bamboos and reeds, temples shaded by immense cedars; rose-tinted children, Buddhist priests, and Confucian sectaries

q: What was Passepartout's reaction to the bridge stunt as it happened?
a: He was astounded; he thought it 'a little too American' but, when challenged by Proctor and called afraid, declared 'a Frenchman can be as American as they.'

q: What was the daily railway construction speed on the prairies?
a: a mile and a half a day — a locomotive ran on rails laid the evening before, brought new rails for the morrow, and advanced as fast as they were laid

q: How did Passepartout escape his fate as a starving foreigner?
a: He saw a placard for 'LONG NOSES! UNDER THE DIRECT PATRONAGE OF THE GOD TINGOU!' — an Acrobatic Japanese Troupe leaving for the United States — and joined Mr. Batulcar's troupe.

q: What was the highest elevation reached by the Pacific Railroad on Fogg's route?
a: Evans Pass — eight thousand and ninety-two feet above sea level

q: How many miles had been covered from San Francisco in three days and nights?
a: thirteen hundred and eighty-two miles

q: What is the capital of Colorado, and what makes the country around rich?
a: Denver — the country is rich in gold and silver, with more than fifty thousand inhabitants already settled there

q: What boundary does Lodge Pole Creek mark?
a: the boundary between the territories of Wyoming and Colorado

q: When was the Union Pacific Railroad inaugurated, and by whom?
a: the 23rd of October, 1867, by the chief engineer General Dodge

q: Where did the Union Pacific inauguration take place?
a: Julesburg, on the southern branch of the Platte River

q: Who was vice-president of the road at the inauguration?
a: Thomas C. Durant

q: What was published at the Union Pacific inauguration?
a: the first number of the Railway Pioneer, printed by a press brought on the train

q: What ceremonies marked the Union Pacific inauguration?
a: cheers were given, the Sioux and Pawnees performed an imitation Indian battle, and fireworks were let off

q: How does Verne describe the locomotive's whistle?
a: more powerful than Amphion's lyre — about to bid cities and towns rise from American soil

q: How far was Omaha from Fort McPherson?
a: three hundred and fifty-seven miles

q: What town was Passepartout's train approaching when the 101st meridian was passed?
a: North Platte, built between the two arms of the Platte River

q: What card did Fogg play that triggered Colonel Proctor's quarrel?
a: the ten of spades — Proctor had insisted he should play a diamond

q: What did Colonel Proctor call Fogg during the whist quarrel?
a: son of John Bull

q: Where did Fogg agree to fight Colonel Proctor?
a: Plum Creek — the next station, where the train would stop ten minutes

q: Why did the duel at Plum Creek not take place?
a: the train was twenty minutes late and would not stop — the conductor refused to let the duellists off

q: What alternative location did the conductor propose for the duel?
a: the last car of the moving train, vacated by a dozen passengers — the duellists would march on each other in the aisle and fire at the first whistle

q: How were the duellists armed?
a: each provided with two six-barrelled revolvers

q: What interrupted the duel-on-the-train?
a: a band of Sioux Indians attacked the train

q: How many Sioux jumped onto the train?
a: a hundred

q: What mistake did the Sioux chief make at the locomotive?
a: He opened the steam-valve wide instead of closing it — the locomotive plunged forward with terrific velocity.

q: How did Aouda behave during the Sioux raid?
a: She defended herself like a true heroine with a revolver, shooting through the broken windows whenever a savage made his appearance.

q: How far was the train from Fort Kearney station when the conductor warned of disaster?
a: two miles

q: What did Passepartout do to save the train?
a: He slipped under the cars, worked his way to the front using old acrobatic skills, and loosened the safety chains between the baggage-car and the tender, detaching the locomotive.

q: How close did the detached train stop to Fort Kearney station?
a: less than a hundred feet

q: How many passengers were missing after the Sioux raid?
a: three — including Passepartout

q: Who was seriously wounded among the passengers in the Sioux raid?
a: Colonel Proctor — a ball had entered his groin

q: Was Fix wounded in the Sioux raid?
a: slightly — in the arm

q: What was Fogg's resolution after the Sioux raid?
a: I will find him, living or dead — even though sacrificing this delay would lose the steamer at New York and certainly lose his bet

q: What reward did Fogg offer the Fort Kearney soldiers?
a: five thousand dollars, divided among them, if they saved the prisoners

q: How many volunteers did the captain of Fort Kearney provide?
a: thirty — led by an old sergeant

q: Who did Fogg ask Fix to stay with at Fort Kearney?
a: Aouda — 'in case anything should happen to me'

q: How did the missing locomotive return to Fort Kearney?
a: The engineer and stoker came to from being stunned, found themselves alone with a stopped locomotive twenty miles past the fort, rebuilt the fire, and ran backwards to Fort Kearney.

q: What did Aouda do when the train was ready to start without Fogg?
a: She refused to go: 'I will not go.' She and Fix stayed behind.

q: How far south of Fort Kearney did Fogg's squad fight the Indians?
a: ten miles

q: What did Passepartout do as the soldiers approached?
a: He had begun to struggle with his captors, three of whom he felled with his fists, when his master and the soldiers hastened up to their relief.

q: How many hours behind was Fogg after the rescue?
a: twenty hours behind time

q: What was Passepartout's wry reflection after the rescue?
a: It must certainly be confessed that I cost my master dear!

q: Who proposed the sledge ride to Omaha?
a: Fix — based on a man who had spoken to him during the night

q: What was the name of the sledge owner?
a: Mudge

q: Describe the sledge.
a: a kind of frame on two long beams raised in front like the runners of a sledge, room for five or six persons, a high mast with brigantine sail and jib, and a rudder for steering — rigged like a sloop

q: How fast did Mudge's sledge travel?
a: forty miles an hour

q: How far was Fort Kearney to Omaha by air?
a: at most two hundred miles

q: What time did the sledge depart Fort Kearney?
a: eight o'clock

q: What two dangers did Fogg fear on the sledge journey?
a: an accident to the sledge, and a change or calm in the wind

q: What animals chased the sledge?
a: bands of gaunt, famished, ferocious prairie-wolves — Passepartout held a revolver ready to fire

q: What musical observation did Fogg make about the sledge's wires?
a: the metallic lashings, like the chords of a stringed instrument, gave the fifth and the octave

q: When did the sledge reach Omaha?
a: Mudge said 'We have got there!' — about an hour after crossing the Platte River, mid-afternoon

q: What route did the train from Omaha to Chicago take?
a: the Chicago and Rock Island Railroad — running directly east, passing fifty stations

q: What states did the train cross from Omaha to Chicago?
a: Iowa — by Council Bluffs, Des Moines, and Iowa City — then crossed the Mississippi at Davenport, and entered Illinois by Rock Island

q: When did the train reach Chicago?
a: the 10th, at four o'clock in the evening

q: What detail does Verne mention about Chicago?
a: already risen from its ruins, more proudly seated than ever on the borders of its beautiful Lake Michigan

q: How far is Chicago from New York?
a: nine hundred miles

q: What railway took Fogg from Chicago to New York?
a: the Pittsburgh, Fort Wayne, and Chicago Railway

q: When did Fogg arrive at the New York station, and which one?
a: a quarter-past eleven in the evening of the 11th, on the right bank of the Hudson, before the very pier of the Cunard line

q: What was the name of the steamer Fogg missed at New York?
a: the China — for Liverpool — had started three-quarters of an hour before

q: Why couldn't Fogg use the Pereire of the French Transatlantic Company?
a: It did not leave until the 14th — too late for his deadline.

q: Why couldn't Fogg use the Hamburg boats?
a: They went directly to Havre, not Liverpool or London — the additional trip from Havre to Southampton would render Fogg's last efforts of no avail.

q: Why couldn't Fogg use the Inman steamer?
a: It did not depart till the next day, and could not cross the Atlantic in time to save the wager.

q: Where did the party stay in New York?
a: the St. Nicholas Hotel, on Broadway

q: What was the date Fogg woke at the St. Nicholas?
a: December 12th — nine days, thirteen hours, and forty-five minutes to the deadline

q: What is the name of the trading vessel Fogg discovered at the Battery?
a: the Henrietta — iron-hulled, wood-built above

q: Describe Captain Andrew Speedy of the Henrietta.
a: a man of fifty, a sort of sea-wolf, with big eyes, a complexion of oxidised copper, red hair and thick neck, and a growling voice

q: Where was Captain Speedy from?
a: Cardiff

q: Where was the Henrietta bound originally?
a: Bordeaux

q: What was the Henrietta's speed?
a: between eleven and twelve knots

q: Did Captain Speedy take passengers normally?
a: No — 'No passengers. Never have passengers. Too much in the way.'

q: How much did Fogg offer per person to go to Bordeaux?
a: two thousand dollars apiece for four people — eight thousand dollars total

q: When did the Henrietta sail from New York?
a: nine o'clock in the morning of December 12th

q: What did Fogg do once the Henrietta left port?
a: He bribed the sailors and stokers to mutiny in his favour, locked Captain Speedy in his cabin, and turned the vessel toward Liverpool — proving he had been a sailor.

q: How long had Fogg been on board before the takeover?
a: about thirty hours

q: What date did the Henrietta pass the Banks of Newfoundland?
a: the 13th of December

q: What direction did the wind veer when the Henrietta hit weather trouble?
a: southeast — making the sails useless

q: What problem did the engineer report to Fogg on December 16th?
a: There was not enough coal to go with all steam from New York to Liverpool — only enough for short steam to Bordeaux.

q: What was Fogg's solution to the coal shortage?
a: Feed all the fires until the coal is exhausted — then burn the upper part of the vessel itself.

q: How much did Fogg pay Captain Speedy to buy the Henrietta?
a: sixty thousand dollars

q: What did Fogg burn first as fuel after coal ran out?
a: the interior seats, bunks, and frames — the poop, cabins, bunks, and spare deck were sacrificed on day 18

q: What did Fogg burn on December 19th?
a: the masts, rafts, and spars — the crew worked lustily, keeping up the fires

q: What was left of the Henrietta on December 20th?
a: only a flat hulk — railings, fittings, the greater part of the deck, and top sides had disappeared

q: What lighthouse did the Henrietta sight on the 20th?
a: Fastnet Light — they passed Queenstown by ten in the evening

q: What was Fogg's gambit at Queenstown?
a: Queenstown was the Irish port where transatlantic steamers put off mails, then carried by express train to Dublin and on to Liverpool by fast boats — gaining twelve hours on the Atlantic steamers.

q: When did the Henrietta enter Queenstown Harbour?
a: one o'clock in the morning, at high tide

q: What did Fogg do to Captain Speedy at Queenstown?
a: He left Speedy on the levelled hulk of his craft, which was still worth half what he had sold it for, with a hearty handshake.

q: When did Fogg disembark on the Liverpool quay?
a: twenty minutes before twelve, the 21st of December

q: How far was Liverpool from London?
a: six hours

q: What did Fix do as Fogg disembarked at Liverpool?
a: He put his hand on Fogg's shoulder, showed his warrant, and said: 'I arrest you in the Queen's name!'

q: Where was Fogg held after his arrest in Liverpool?
a: the Custom House — to be transferred to London the next day

q: How long did Fogg have between his arrival and the deadline?
a: nine hours and a quarter — from 11:40 a.m. to a quarter before nine in the evening; the journey to London was six hours

q: What did Fogg write in his journal at the Custom House?
a: On the line '21st December, Saturday, Liverpool,' he added: '80th day, 11.40 a.m.'

q: What time did the Custom House clock strike vs. Fogg's watch?
a: the clock struck one — Fogg's watch was two hours too fast

q: Why was Fogg released from the Custom House?
a: Fix discovered the real bank robber, James Strand, had been arrested three days earlier at Edinburgh — Fogg was free.

q: What did Fogg do upon being told he was free?
a: He walked to Fix and, with the only rapid motion he had ever made in his life, knocked Fix down with the precision of a machine.

q: What did Passepartout exclaim after Fogg knocked Fix down?
a: Well hit! Parbleu! that's what you might call a good application of English fists!

q: When did Fogg leave Liverpool by special train?
a: three o'clock — the express train had left thirty-five minutes before

q: When did Fogg's special train arrive in London?
a: ten minutes before nine — five minutes too late, all London clocks were striking the time

q: Per Fogg's understanding, when had he arrived in London?
a: Saturday, December 21st at 8:50 PM — five minutes past the deadline; he had lost the wager

q: What did Passepartout do upon returning to Saville Row?
a: He extinguished the gas burner that had been burning for eighty days — finding a bill from the gas company in the letter-box

q: What did Fogg's friends do to make him pay the bet?
a: His five Reform Club antagonists already had his cheque; they only had to fill it out and send it to Barings to have the £20,000 transferred.

q: Did Mr. Fogg sleep the night after his loss?
a: Mr. Fogg went to bed but Aouda did not once close her eyes; Passepartout watched all night, like a faithful dog, at his master's door.

q: What did Fogg ask Passepartout to bring him for breakfast?
a: a cup of tea and a chop

q: Whom did Aouda propose marriage to, and how?
a: Phileas Fogg — 'Mr. Fogg, do you wish at once a kinswoman and friend? Will you have me for your wife?'

q: What was Fogg's reply to Aouda's proposal?
a: I love you! Yes, by all that is holiest, I love you, and I am entirely yours!

q: Whom did Passepartout fetch to perform the wedding?
a: the Reverend Samuel Wilson, of Marylebone parish

q: What time was the wedding initially set?
a: for to-morrow, Monday — Mr. Fogg believed it was Saturday evening at 8:05 PM

q: What was Aouda's reaction to Fogg's offer to put his last money at her disposal?
a: She asked his forgiveness for having followed him and perhaps contributed to his ruin; instead of accepting, she proposed marriage.

q: When was the real bank robber arrested?
a: the 17th of December — at Edinburgh — three days before Fogg returned to London

q: What was the real robber's name?
a: James Strand

q: How did public opinion respond when Strand was arrested?
a: Phileas Fogg's name was once more at a premium on 'Change; the bonds went from 100 below par to 20, then 10, then 5; even paralytic Lord Albemarle bet again in his favour.

q: Who waited at the Reform Club on Saturday December 21st?
a: the five whist partners: John Sullivan, Samuel Fallentin, Andrew Stuart, Gauthier Ralph, and Thomas Flanagan

q: What time was the last Liverpool train to arrive that day?
a: twenty-three minutes past seven — the next was at ten minutes after twelve, both before and after the deadline

q: How did Fogg make his entrance at the Reform Club?
a: At the fifty-seventh second of the sixtieth minute, the door of the saloon opened; the pendulum had not beat the sixtieth second when Phileas Fogg appeared, followed by an excited crowd, and said: 'Here I am, gentlemen!'

q: What did Andrew Stuart say at the moment when the bet seemed lost?
a: I wouldn't give up my four thousand of the bet for three thousand nine hundred and ninety-nine.

q: Why did Fogg believe he had arrived a day late when he had not?
a: He had travelled constantly eastward and gained one day — 360 degrees × 4 minutes per degree = 24 hours; while Fogg's eastward journey saw the sun cross the meridian eighty times, his friends in London only saw it cross seventy-nine times.

q: What was the actual day Fogg arrived in London?
a: Friday, the 20th of December — the seventy-ninth day from his departure, not the eightieth

q: How did Passepartout discover the date error?
a: He went to engage Reverend Samuel Wilson at 8:05 PM for a Monday wedding; Wilson said the wedding would have to be 'tomorrow' (Sunday); Passepartout realised they had arrived twenty-four hours ahead of time.

q: What did Passepartout cry when he ran back to Fogg?
a: Yes, yes, yes, yes! You have made a mistake of one day! We arrived twenty-four hours ahead of time; but there are only ten minutes left!

q: What did Passepartout's family watch keep that would have caught the error?
a: London time only — but it did not mark the days as well as the hours and minutes, so it could not betray the gain

q: How did Fogg get from Saville Row to the Reform Club after Passepartout's news?
a: He jumped into a cab, promised a hundred pounds to the cabman, ran over two dogs, and overturned five carriages, reaching the Reform Club at a quarter before nine.

q: How much did Fogg's tour cost in total?
a: nearly nineteen thousand pounds — out of his £20,000 winnings, only £1,000 remained as net pecuniary gain

q: What did Fogg do with the £1,000 remaining after the wager?
a: He divided it between Passepartout and Fix — deducting from Passepartout's share the cost of the gas which had burned for nineteen hundred and twenty hours, for the sake of regularity.

q: How long burned the gas in Passepartout's room?
a: nineteen hundred and twenty hours — about eighty days

q: When did Fogg and Aouda's wedding take place?
a: forty-eight hours after their reunion — Passepartout, glowing and dazzling, gave the bride away

q: What every-means-of-conveyance list does Verne offer for Fogg's journey?
a: steamers, railways, carriages, yachts, trading-vessels, sledges, elephants

q: What gain does Verne say Fogg made by the trip?
a: Nothing but a charming woman who, strange as it may appear, made him the happiest of men.

q: What did Passepartout discover after the wedding regarding the trip?
a: We might have made the tour of the world in only seventy-eight days — by not crossing India.

q: Fogg's reply to the 78-day observation?
a: No doubt, by not crossing India. But if I had not crossed India, I should not have saved Aouda; she would not have been my wife, and—

q: What date did Fogg actually return to London?
a: Friday, the 20th of December, at 8:50 PM (in his own count) — but actually before the 8:45 PM Saturday deadline because of the gained day

q: What did Andrew Stuart say about Fogg when minutes seemed long?
a: Why, if I should see him, I should not believe it was he.

q: What was Lord Albemarle's bet just before Fogg's return?
a: five thousand pounds in Fogg's favour — paralytic old Lord Albemarle bet even in his favour as Fogg drew near his last turning-point

q: What did Mr. Fogg's Bradshaw consult tell him about transatlantic options?
a: Bradshaw gave the daily movements of transatlantic steamers — but the Pereire, Hamburg boats, and Inman steamer were all unsuitable.

q: What was Captain Speedy's reaction to Fogg taking command?
a: He howled and growled in his cabin; Passepartout took the greatest precautions when carrying him meals.

q: What was Fix's state of mind during the sledge journey?
a: He was resolved to do his duty and hasten the return to England; his opinion of Fogg was somewhat modified, but he kept the warrant in his pocket.

q: How did Fix struggle with himself over Fogg's heroic departure?
a: He saw the folly of letting Fogg go alone — the man whom he had followed around the world was permitted to separate himself; he 'began to accuse and abuse himself' and called himself an idiot.

q: What did Andrew Speedy call Fogg as a high compliment after the bargain?
a: Captain Fogg, you've got something of the Yankee about you.

q: Why was a stranger asked to perform the marriage?
a: the Reverend Samuel Wilson of Marylebone parish — Passepartout went to engage him at five minutes past eight in the evening

q: What did Fogg do to the captain's vessel after buying it?
a: He had the interior seats, bunks, and frames pulled down and burned to keep up steam pressure as coal ran out.

q: Why did Fogg consult Bradshaw at New York?
a: to find any transatlantic steamer that could reach Liverpool in time — but the Pereire (14th), Hamburg boats (Havre), and Inman steamer all failed to fit

q: How long did the discussion at the Reform Club take before Fogg's arrival?
a: the five gentlemen were waiting from before 8:20 PM — at 8:55 PM (twenty minutes to nine, then five minutes more) the saloon door opened just before the sixtieth second

q: What did Mudge say about reaching Omaha?
a: If nothing breaks, we shall get there!

q: What did the captain of Fort Kearney first say about Fogg's request to chase the Sioux?
a: Doubtless; but can I risk the lives of fifty men to save three?

q: What touched the captain into providing volunteers?
a: Fogg's calm announcement: 'Very well, I will go alone' — the captain was touched in spite of himself

q: Why did Fogg engage the engineer at Liverpool?
a: He had ordered a special train to London, stimulating the engineer with the offer of a generous reward — needed to make the journey in five and a half hours

q: What was the difference between haggling at New York and at Hong Kong?
a: At New York money failed — Captain Speedy refused both freight and purchase; up to that point, money had smoothed every obstacle.

q: What surprise greeted Passepartout the morning after Aouda's proposal?
a: He discovered they had a 24-hour gain by going east — they were a day early, not a day late.

q: How did Fogg distribute reward at Fort Kearney?
a: He distributed the £1,000-equivalent ($5,000) reward among the soldiers who rescued Passepartout

q: How many sailors did Fogg bribe to take the Henrietta?
a: the entire occasional crew, who were not on the best terms with the captain — they went over to him in a body

q: Why was Fix on the verge of an apoplectic fit during the Henrietta deal?
a: Nearly twenty thousand pounds had been spent — Fogg left the hull and engine to the captain (near the whole value of the craft); the £55,000 stolen from the Bank was being squandered.

q: What was Aouda's role in the Sioux raid?
a: She defended herself courageously like a heroine with a revolver — twenty Sioux fell mortally wounded while she fired through broken windows.

q: What were the Reform Club bonds quoted at as Fogg approached the deadline?
a: no longer at a hundred below par, but at twenty, at ten, and at five

q: What did Fogg pay Captain Speedy in dollars?
a: sixty thousand dollars — Speedy's vessel was worth fifty thousand

q: What detail did Mr. Fogg make about the wires of the sledge mast?
a: He observed they gave the fifth and the octave — the only words he uttered during the journey.

q: What ironic discovery did Passepartout make in his room on returning?
a: He found a bill from the gas company for the burner that had been on for eighty days — he extinguished it for good.

q: How did Aouda spend the night while waiting for Fogg's return?
a: She wandered about on the verge of the plains, full of sad forebodings, her heart stifled with anguish, despite the cold.

q: What was Fogg's net reaction to the discovery he was actually a day early?
a: He had Passepartout summon Reverend Samuel Wilson, took a cab to the Reform Club, and arrived with the calm 'Here I am, gentlemen!' at the fifty-seventh second of the sixtieth.

q: What was Fix's reflection on Fogg's spending?
a: The Bank of England would certainly not come out of this affair well indemnified — more than seven thousand pounds would have been spent by the time they reached England.

q: What presentiment did Fix have about Fogg seizing the Henrietta?
a: A man who began by stealing fifty-five thousand pounds might end by stealing a vessel; Fix suspected Fogg, turned pirate, was heading not to Liverpool but to some safe harbour.

q: What was the precision of Fogg's punch on Fix?
a: The only rapid motion he had ever made in his life — drew back his arms and with the precision of a machine knocked Fix down.

q: Who was the only friend who held out for Fogg's possible arrival?
a: Samuel Fallentin — 'You know that Mr. Fogg is very eccentric. His punctuality is well known; he never arrives too soon, or too late; and I should not be surprised if he appeared before us at the last minute.'

q: What did Fogg's distribution of $5,000 reward become?
a: His promise to divide five thousand dollars among the soldiers if they saved the prisoners — fulfilled after the rescue.

q: How long had the gas been burning in Passepartout's Saville Row room?
a: eighty days — extinguished on Fogg's return to London; gas company bill in the letter-box

q: Did Fogg gain anything from his journey?
a: Yes — a charming woman; the pecuniary gain was small (about £1,000 of his £20,000), but he gained Aouda, who made him the happiest of men.

q: Where had Phileas Fogg been on the seventy-sixth day of his journey, December 17th?
a: still on the Atlantic crossing — no news had been received in London, and the police were ignorant of Fix's whereabouts

q: Why couldn't Fogg use the Inman steamer at New York?
a: It did not depart till the next day, and could not cross the Atlantic in time to save the wager.

q: What was Phileas Fogg's calm response to Colonel Proctor about appointing the duel for six months hence?
a: Why not ten years hence? — Proctor said. Fogg replied 'I say six months, and I shall be at the place of meeting promptly.'

q: When did Fogg arrive at the Liverpool quay?
a: twenty minutes before twelve, on the 21st of December

q: What was the deciding moment at the Reform Club?
a: At the fifty-seventh second of the sixtieth, the saloon door opened — Phileas Fogg appeared with the calm 'Here I am, gentlemen!' before the pendulum beat the sixtieth second.

q: What was Mr. Fogg's response to the seventy-eight-day observation?
a: He shut the door — implicitly 'No' — saying that without crossing India he would not have saved Aouda.

q: How was the squad rescued?
a: At dawn, gunshots were heard half a mile from the fort; soldiers rushed out and saw Fogg's little band returning in good order — Passepartout and the other rescued travellers in the middle.

q: What did Mr. Fogg say when Passepartout cried 'why do you not curse me?' over the missed steamer?
a: I blame no one. Go!

q: What time did the sledge depart Fort Kearney for Omaha?
a: eight o'clock in the morning

q: What date did Fogg actually arrive in London at 8:50 PM?
a: Friday, December 20th — but he believed it was Saturday December 21st, due to the gained day from eastward travel

q: What was Fix's psychological state during Captain Speedy's payment?
a: He seemed on the point of having an apoplectic fit — nearly £20,000 had been expended, and Fogg left the hull/engine to the captain

q: What was the engineer's report about the coal supply on the Henrietta?
a: There was enough coal to go on short steam from New York to Bordeaux, but not enough to go with all steam from New York to Liverpool.

q: What was Passepartout's despair at being late?
a: He thought he had ruined his master — twenty hours behind, with the wager apparently lost.

q: What did the sledge cross by chord rather than arc?
a: the Platte River route via Great Island, Columbus, Schuyler, Fremont — the railway took an arc; the sledge took a straight chord

q: What was Aouda's response when Fogg said 'I have no relatives'?
a: I pity you, then, Mr. Fogg, for solitude is a sad thing, with no heart to which to confide your griefs. They say, though, that misery itself, shared by two sympathetic souls, may be borne with patience.

q: What gain did Verne mention as 'precisely twenty-four hours' on the journey?
a: Three hundred and sixty degrees × four minutes per degree = twenty-four hours — the day unconsciously gained by going eastward

q: What was the bond price right before Fogg's return?
a: Phileas Fogg bonds, no longer at a hundred below par, but at twenty, at ten, and at five — Lord Albemarle bet even in his favour

q: What was Passepartout's view of Fogg's conquest of the Henrietta?
a: He thought Mr. Fogg's manœuvre simply glorious — formed warm friendships with the sailors and amazed them with his acrobatic feats.
